steps.title: Identify what the question needs
steps.content: The passage notes that chimpanzees' vitamin D levels appeared unaffected by oral supplementation, but then qualifies this with ‘however, supplementation was rare, highly varied, and poorly tracked.’ The question asks what conclusion the researchers can most logically draw given that qualifier.

steps.title: Eliminate the choices
steps.content:
• "the effect of supplemental vitamin D on zoo chimpanzees in Spain and other mid-latitude countries can more clearly be observed in summer than in autumn.": The passage says nothing about summer being clearer for observation; this invents a comparison not supported by the data. ✗
  • "the possibility that zoo chimpanzees in Spain and other mid-latitude countries would benefit from supplemental vitamin D during autumn cannot be excluded.": When supplementation is rare, highly varied, and poorly tracked, the study cannot confidently rule out a possible benefit from supplementation. The benefit during autumn remains an open question. ✓
  • "vitamin D levels in zoo chimpanzees in Spain and other mid-latitude countries may be higher during autumn than Moretti and colleagues' data appear to indicate.": Poor tracking of supplementation does not show that the measured vitamin D levels themselves were inaccurate; it only limits what can be concluded about supplementation's effects. ✗
  • "differences in vitamin D supplementation likely explain more of the difference in vitamin D levels across zoo chimpanzees in Spain and other mid-latitude countries than seasonal differences in UVB irradiation do.": The poor supplementation data makes it impossible to support such a strong causal claim; this reverses the relationship the passage actually suggests. ✗
That matches the correct answer — B is correct.
